title: The Human-AI Interaction Bias Codex
subtitle: A denser Wikipedia-backed map of human-AI interaction distortions, including interaction-relevant cognitive and algorithmic bias terms.
mission: Curate a Wikipedia-backed taxonomy of human judgment distortions in human-AI interaction, including interaction-relevant human cognitive biases and AI-system biases.

curated_table.why_it_fits: This is the canonical human-AI overtrust page: users overweight automated outputs and underweight independent checks.
Section-backed replacement for automation misuse: it directly names the misuse branch of automation failure in a canonical Wikipedia source.
Section-backed replacement for algorithm appreciation: it captures the concrete error pattern that occurs when users defer too readily to automated output.
Useful for overdependence on AI when repeated reliance weakens the user’s willingness to think or act independently.
Explains why expert framing, system labels, or confident AI delivery can trigger deference.
Fits when users accept AI output because it sounds authoritative rather than because they evaluated the evidence.
Belongs here because polished, smooth AI answers can feel more trustworthy even when they are not more accurate.
More specific than generic credibility: it captures how users accept or reject AI output based on the perceived trustworthiness of the source.
Directly describes what happens when users supervise AI less actively after they come to trust it.
Fits because AI-assisted workflows often still require careful monitoring that users let lapse.
Belongs here when AI tools weaken the user’s active grasp of the task by taking over too much monitoring.
Fits the trust-failure pattern where users lose track of what the automation is doing and are surprised by its behavior.
This is the clearest page for under-trust and backlash against AI assistance.
Fits when people resist AI recommendations because the system feels intrusive or controlling.
A real Wikipedia page for advice-taking research that fits under AI distrust when users underweight external machine advice relative to their own judgment.
More specific than generic trust: it captures the user-side acceptance process that shapes whether people rely on AI systems at all.
Included here as an interaction-relevant legitimacy concept: users calibrate trust partly through whether a system appears fair or biased.
Fits the felt-control side of AI reliance: people respond differently when they experience the system as controlling outcomes rather than supporting their own agency.
Core page for why users turn to AI to validate prior beliefs and read its outputs selectively.
Fits when users accept agreeable AI answers and rationalize away the rest.
Useful when plausible-sounding AI output is accepted because it fits a prior belief, not because the reasoning is strong.
Belongs here because users may see AI or other people as biased while overlooking their own selective uptake of AI output.
Directly maps to prompting, searching, and iterating with AI until it yields a preferred answer.
Fits when users highlight the parts of an AI answer that confirm their view and ignore the rest.
Belongs here because AI contradiction often fails to change a user’s position once they are committed to it.
Fits when users pull validating examples from AI output while discarding counterexamples or caveats.
Belongs here because AI-mediated feeds and conversational loops can repeatedly surface the same viewpoint.
Fits personalization-driven AI systems that keep serving what already matches the user’s profile or history.
Useful when AI restates or rephrases a claim often enough that it feels more established.
Fits when AI systems amplify already-circulating narratives until they seem self-validating.
More specific than a generic stereotype page: it captures the structured warmth-competence template through which AI outputs can reinforce social impressions.
Fits when AI outputs trigger or reinforce unconscious group associations rather than explicit reasoning.
Useful for understanding how people and systems flatten diverse groups into overly uniform categories.
More specific than a generic schema page: AI outputs can feel right when they match a familiar type or stereotype, even when the evidence is weak.
Included here because biased AI outputs can feed back into user beliefs and make historical patterns look objective.
Fits when an AI system appears to confirm a story because the underlying data were skewed from the start.
Belongs here because non-representative training or evaluation data can make AI outputs reinforce a partial view of the world.
A better Wikipedia-backed fit for AI measurement problems: systems often optimize proxies that users then mistake for the real variable of interest.

curated_table.why_it_fits: Belongs here because surfaced examples and highlighted cases can steer decisions more than base rates.

curated_table.why_it_fits: Directly captures how AI wording and presentation alter judgments without changing the underlying information.

curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits when AI outputs persuade by being cleanly phrased and cognitively easy rather than substantively better.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Belongs here because repeated interface exposure can make certain options feel safer or more appealing.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Useful when AI surfaces vivid anecdotes or examples that outweigh broader evidence in decision-making.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits AI interfaces that prefill, pre-rank, or preselect an answer path for the user.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users often stay with the system’s initial setup rather than reconsider it.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Useful as the umbrella concept for AI interfaces quietly structuring judgment and action.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits recommendation sets where AI-added comparisons steer the user toward a target option.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits AI interfaces that present many generated or ranked options, making overload itself part of how the system steers the user.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Useful when AI presentation makes an option look stronger or weaker by changing the comparison frame.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits deliberate AI interface design that steers user behavior while preserving nominal choice.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Belongs here because highlighted interface elements and repeated signals dominate user attention.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits prompt wording, examples, and prior AI turns that shift later decisions before reflection.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Useful when popularity cues, consensus counts, or “others chose this” signals make AI options feel correct.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits conversational AI contexts where the latest answer or last-ranked item crowds out better earlier evidence.
Core page for treating AI systems as if they had human traits or inner lives.
Directly names the AI-specific version of the human-likeness attribution problem.
More specific than the linguistics page on animacy: lifelike motion cues in robots and avatars can make AI seem animate and intentional.
Belongs here because near-human AI cues can intensify mind-perception errors rather than simply reducing them.
This is the canonical page for mistaking conversational smoothness for actual understanding.
Fits human-AI interaction directly: users respond socially to systems that only simulate social cues.
Belongs here because AI chat systems can feel socially present in ways that distort user judgment.
Useful for explaining why people read motives into AI output that only reflects patterns and system design.
Fits when ordinary mind-reading habits are extended to AI systems that do not actually possess human-like minds.
Belongs here because users may infer a deliberate agent behind ambiguous AI behavior or outputs.
Fits when users project intention or understanding onto AI as if those impressions were intrinsic system properties.
Useful when users read emotion, purpose, or message into ambiguous machine output.
Belongs here because users often infer hidden depth or intent from coincidental AI behavior.
Fits when users fill in unknown details about AI with oversimplified or incorrect internal models.
Useful when users become too confident in their own story about how the AI reached an answer.
Fits the tendency to mistake a convincing AI explanation for genuine deep understanding by either the user or the system.
Belongs here because users can become too certain about what an AI knows or can do.
Fits when prompt tweaking or interface interaction makes users think they understand and control the AI more than they do.
Included here as an interaction-relevant illusion risk: explanation can improve oversight, but it can also create a false sense that the system is fully understood.

pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Human-like feelings, intentions, or behavioral traits are attributed specifically to AI systems.
pages.why_it_fits: Directly names the AI-specific version of the human-likeness attribution problem.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Biological motion is movement that humans perceive as coming from a living organism.
pages.why_it_fits: More specific than the linguistics page on animacy: lifelike motion cues in robots and avatars can make AI seem animate and intentional.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: The uncanny valley effect describes strong responses to entities that appear almost human but not fully so.
pages.why_it_fits: Belongs here because near-human AI cues can intensify mind-perception errors rather than simply reducing them.

wedges.public_label: Conversation makes software feel socially present

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: People can attribute understanding or empathy to a conversational program beyond what it warrants.
pages.why_it_fits: This is the canonical page for mistaking conversational smoothness for actual understanding.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: People often apply human social rules and expectations to computers and software agents.
pages.why_it_fits: Fits human-AI interaction directly: users respond socially to systems that only simulate social cues.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Digital interfaces can create a stronger sense that another social presence is really there.
pages.why_it_fits: Belongs here because AI chat systems can feel socially present in ways that distort user judgment.

wedges.public_label: We read goals, minds, and motives into AI behavior

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Behavior is interpreted by ascribing beliefs, goals, and intentions to an entity.
pages.why_it_fits: Useful for explaining why people read motives into AI output that only reflects patterns and system design.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Theory of mind is the capacity to understand others by ascribing mental states to them.
pages.why_it_fits: Fits when ordinary mind-reading habits are extended to AI systems that do not actually possess human-like minds.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Agent detection is the inclination to presume purposeful intervention by an intelligent or sentient agent.
pages.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users may infer a deliberate agent behind ambiguous AI behavior or outputs.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: A person mistakes subjective judgments or interpretations for objective properties of the world.
pages.why_it_fits: Fits when users project intention or understanding onto AI as if those impressions were intrinsic system properties.

wedges.public_label: We see patterns and coherence that the system does not have

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Meaningful patterns or agency are perceived in ambiguous stimuli where none may exist.
pages.why_it_fits: Useful when users read emotion, purpose, or message into ambiguous machine output.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Apophenia is the tendency to perceive meaningful connections between unrelated things.
pages.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users often infer hidden depth or intent from coincidental AI behavior.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: A mental model is an internal representation of how a system works.
pages.why_it_fits: Fits when users fill in unknown details about AI with oversimplified or incorrect internal models.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: People often overestimate the accuracy of their own explanations for their judgments and mental processes.
pages.why_it_fits: Useful when users become too confident in their own story about how the AI reached an answer.

wedges.public_label: We overestimate how deeply AI actually understands

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: People often believe they understand a topic more deeply than they actually do.
pages.why_it_fits: Fits the tendency to mistake a convincing AI explanation for genuine deep understanding by either the user or the system.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Explainable AI is a field concerned with making AI reasoning or behavior more understandable to humans.
pages.why_it_fits: Included here as an interaction-relevant illusion risk: explanation can improve oversight, but it can also create a false sense that the system is fully understood.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: People can overestimate how much control they have over events and systems.
pages.why_it_fits: Fits when prompt tweaking or interface interaction makes users think they understand and control the AI more than they do.

pages.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
pages.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
pages.one_sentence_definition: Subjective confidence is often greater than objective accuracy.
pages.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users can become too certain about what an AI knows or can do.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: This section of the automation bias literature distinguishes ignoring automation from overusing it in the wrong conditions.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Section-backed replacement for automation misuse: it directly names the misuse branch of automation failure in a canonical Wikipedia source.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: This section describes mistakes made by following bad automated advice or by failing to act when the system stays silent.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Section-backed replacement for algorithm appreciation: it captures the concrete error pattern that occurs when users defer too readily to automated output.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Repeated experiences of lacking control can reduce initiative and independent action.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful for overdependence on AI when repeated reliance weakens the user’s willingness to think or act independently.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The tendency to give extra weight to opinions or outputs associated with authority.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Explains why expert framing, system labels, or confident AI delivery can trigger deference.
cross_links: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A form of reasoning that treats an authority’s claim as evidence that the claim is true.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when users accept AI output because it sounds authoritative rather than because they evaluated the evidence.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Information that is easier to process is often judged as more credible or preferable.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because polished, smooth AI answers can feel more trustworthy even when they are not more accurate.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Source credibility refers to a communicator's qualities that affect whether a receiver accepts a message.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: More specific than generic credibility: it captures how users accept or reject AI output based on the perceived trustworthiness of the source.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Performance can decline when automation removes people from active control and monitoring.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Directly describes what happens when users supervise AI less actively after they come to trust it.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Sustained attention is needed to detect infrequent signals over time.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits because AI-assisted workflows often still require careful monitoring that users let lapse.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The perception and understanding of relevant elements in a changing environment.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here when AI tools weaken the user’s active grasp of the task by taking over too much monitoring.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Unexpected automated behavior that catches the user off guard when the system acts differently than anticipated.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits the trust-failure pattern where users lose track of what the automation is doing and are surprised by its behavior.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The tendency to distrust or reject algorithmic advice, especially after observing mistakes.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: This is the clearest page for under-trust and backlash against AI assistance.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A perceived threat to autonomy can trigger resistance to persuasion or control.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when people resist AI recommendations because the system feels intrusive or controlling.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A judge–advisor system is a decision structure used to study how people take, revise, or discount advice from an adviser.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: A real Wikipedia page for advice-taking research that fits under AI distrust when users underweight external machine advice relative to their own judgment.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The technology acceptance model explains how perceived usefulness and ease of use shape whether people adopt a technology.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: More specific than generic trust: it captures the user-side acceptance process that shapes whether people rely on AI systems at all.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Fairness in machine learning concerns attempts to detect and correct unfair patterns in ML-driven decisions.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Included here as an interaction-relevant legitimacy concept: users calibrate trust partly through whether a system appears fair or biased.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: One positive surface trait can spill over and shape judgment of unrelated traits.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when polished interfaces, strong branding, or articulate wording inflate trust in the entire AI system.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People search for, interpret, and recall information in ways that support what they already believe.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Information is evaluated in ways that serve desired conclusions or identities.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when users accept agreeable AI answers and rationalize away the rest.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Reasoning is judged by whether the conclusion seems believable rather than by whether the logic is sound.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful when plausible-sounding AI output is accepted because it fits a prior belief, not because the reasoning is strong.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People are quicker to detect bias in others than in themselves.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users may see AI or other people as biased while overlooking their own selective uptake of AI output.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People tend to choose information that reinforces their existing views.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Directly maps to prompting, searching, and iterating with AI until it yields a preferred answer.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People more readily notice and remember information that fits existing expectations.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when users highlight the parts of an AI answer that confirm their view and ignore the rest.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A belief can persist even after strong contrary evidence appears.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because AI contradiction often fails to change a user’s position once they are committed to it.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Only the evidence that supports a preferred conclusion is selected while conflicting evidence is ignored.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when users pull validating examples from AI output while discarding counterexamples or caveats.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Beliefs are amplified when repeated inside a closed, like-minded information environment.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because AI-mediated feeds and conversational loops can repeatedly surface the same viewpoint.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Personalized curation can isolate users inside a narrower information environment.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits personalization-driven AI systems that keep serving what already matches the user’s profile or history.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Repeated claims can feel truer even when repetition adds no evidence.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful when AI restates or rephrases a claim often enough that it feels more established.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A belief gains plausibility and spread through repetition and social reinforcement.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when AI systems amplify already-circulating narratives until they seem self-validating.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The stereotype content model explains stereotypes in terms of perceived warmth and competence.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: An implicit stereotype is a pre-reflective attribution of group-linked qualities to others.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Out-group homogeneity is the tendency to see out-group members as more similar to one another than in-group members.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Algorithmic bias describes systematic and repeatable unfair tendencies in computerized sociotechnical systems.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Included here because biased AI outputs can feed back into user beliefs and make historical patterns look objective.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Selection bias is distortion caused by how people, groups, or data are chosen for analysis.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Sampling bias occurs when some members of the intended population are more likely to be included than others.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A proxy variable stands in for an unobservable target and can distort conclusions when it is treated as if it were the real thing.

candidate_inventory.public_label: AI's suggestions steer our choices

candidate_inventory.top_level_category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
candidate_inventory.public_label: AI's suggestions steer our choices

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Easy-to-process information feels more familiar, trustworthy, or true.

candidate_inventory.top_level_category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

candidate_inventory.failure_point: how AI presentation shapes our choices

candidate_inventory.top_level_category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
candidate_inventory.public_label: AI's suggestions steer our choices

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A vivid individual case can evoke stronger judgment than abstract statistics.

candidate_inventory.top_level_category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
candidate_inventory.public_label: AI's suggestions steer our choices

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People disproportionately stick with preselected or default options.

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People prefer maintaining the current or default state of affairs.

candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Earlier stimuli can influence later judgments or responses without conscious intent.

candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Directly names the AI-specific version of the human-likeness attribution problem.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Biological motion is movement that humans perceive as coming from a living organism.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: More specific than the linguistics page on animacy: lifelike motion cues in robots and avatars can make AI seem animate and intentional.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The uncanny valley effect describes strong responses to entities that appear almost human but not fully so.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because near-human AI cues can intensify mind-perception errors rather than simply reducing them.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People can attribute understanding or empathy to a conversational program beyond what it warrants.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: This is the canonical page for mistaking conversational smoothness for actual understanding.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People often apply human social rules and expectations to computers and software agents.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits human-AI interaction directly: users respond socially to systems that only simulate social cues.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Digital interfaces can create a stronger sense that another social presence is really there.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because AI chat systems can feel socially present in ways that distort user judgment.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Behavior is interpreted by ascribing beliefs, goals, and intentions to an entity.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful for explaining why people read motives into AI output that only reflects patterns and system design.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Theory of mind is the capacity to understand others by ascribing mental states to them.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when ordinary mind-reading habits are extended to AI systems that do not actually possess human-like minds.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Agent detection is the inclination to presume purposeful intervention by an intelligent or sentient agent.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users may infer a deliberate agent behind ambiguous AI behavior or outputs.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A person mistakes subjective judgments or interpretations for objective properties of the world.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when users project intention or understanding onto AI as if those impressions were intrinsic system properties.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Meaningful patterns or agency are perceived in ambiguous stimuli where none may exist.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful when users read emotion, purpose, or message into ambiguous machine output.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Apophenia is the tendency to perceive meaningful connections between unrelated things.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users often infer hidden depth or intent from coincidental AI behavior.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: A mental model is an internal representation of how a system works.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when users fill in unknown details about AI with oversimplified or incorrect internal models.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People often overestimate the accuracy of their own explanations for their judgments and mental processes.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful when users become too confident in their own story about how the AI reached an answer.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People often believe they understand a topic more deeply than they actually do.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits the tendency to mistake a convincing AI explanation for genuine deep understanding by either the user or the system.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Subjective confidence is often greater than objective accuracy.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Belongs here because users can become too certain about what an AI knows or can do.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People can overestimate how much control they have over events and systems.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits when prompt tweaking or interface interaction makes users think they understand and control the AI more than they do.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Explainable AI is a field concerned with making AI reasoning or behavior more understandable to humans.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Included here as an interaction-relevant illusion risk: explanation can improve oversight, but it can also create a false sense that the system is fully understood.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: This section names the reduced monitoring and checking that follows when automation feels dependable.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Section-backed support term for the oversight failure that emerges once users stop actively checking automated systems.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: The implicit-association test is a method intended to detect the strength of implicit associations.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful support context for implicit stereotype research, but it is more of a measurement tool than a codex leaf.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: People can misremember where a piece of information originally came from.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful support context because repeated AI phrasing can blur source memory without being a primary human-AI bias leaf.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Choice-supportive bias is the tendency to remember one’s chosen option as better than it was.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful support context when users retrospectively justify following an AI recommendation.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
candidate_inventory.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Personification is the attribution of human form or qualities to an abstract or non-human thing.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Useful support context for human-likeness cues, but broader and less interaction-specific than the visible leaves.

candidate_inventory.public_label: We trust AI too much or too little
candidate_inventory.failure_point: whether we follow AI appropriately
candidate_inventory.one_sentence_definition: Locus of control is the degree to which people believe outcomes depend on their own actions rather than external forces.
candidate_inventory.why_it_fits: Fits the felt-control side of AI reliance: people respond differently when they experience the system as controlling outcomes rather than supporting their own agency.

pages_considered_but_rejected.reason: Important AI failure mode, but it describes model behavior more than a human interaction bias.
Philosophically important, but too abstract and debate-driven for a clean codex leaf.
Historically important, but it is a benchmark frame rather than a recurring human judgment error.
Describes a system type, not a bias or distortion in human judgment by itself.
Too marketing-specific and broad for this codex taxonomy.
Important deployment concept, but mainly a model-performance issue unless tied to a clearer user judgment distortion.
Useful for monitoring systems, but too system-centric to serve as a human-AI bias leaf.
Too procedural and upstream; it explains how datasets are built, not a recurring interaction bias.
A mitigation and evaluation practice rather than a bias concept.
Too broad a design field rather than a specific human judgment distortion.
Relevant to AI bias discussions, but there is no strong standalone English Wikipedia page to use as a canonical leaf here.
No clean canonical English Wikipedia page suitable for a codex leaf, and the concept is too broad as phrased.
